CR1.title: Canonical Root Integrity
CR1.impact: AI systems require a single, unambiguous root domain to identify the source of truth for an entity.
CR1.meaning: The canonical URL must resolve to the same normalized root domain as the site's identity host. Any mismatch weakens authority signals and creates uncertainty about which domain owns the entity.
CR1.remedy: Normalize the canonical URL to match the primary domain root and remove all alternative or cross-domain roots unless purposefully structured.
CR1.risk: AI may treat the entity as fragmented or duplicated, reducing confidence, suppressing ranking, and destabilizing entity identity in search reconstruction.

CR2.title: Schema-Aligned Entity Naming
CR2.impact: Entity identity must remain consistent between the visible brand and the formal schema declaration.
CR2.meaning: The Organization.name and the entityName extracted from the page must match. Divergence implies misalignment between how the brand represents itself and how the structured graph defines it.
CR2.remedy: Ensure Organization.name reflects the true institutional name used in titles, homepages, and canonical references.
CR2.risk: AI models may struggle to reconcile multiple identity tokens, harming trust, interpretability, and name-based entity clustering.

CR3.title: Breadcrumb Root Convergence
CR3.impact: Breadcrumb paths communicate the internal logical structure of an entity's site hierarchy to crawlers.
CR3.meaning: The root of the breadcrumb chain must resolve to the same canonical root as the homepage to signal a coherent information architecture.
CR3.remedy: Ensure the first breadcrumb item resolves to the homepage canonical, not sub-paths, staging URLs, or legacy root domains.
CR3.risk: AI may infer that the site is part of a different structural domain, weakening entity resolution and diminishing hierarchical confidence.

CR4.title: Unified Naming Across Organization Schemas
CR4.impact: Multiple Organization schemas with diverging names fracture institutional identity.
CR4.meaning: All Organization schema instances on the page must declare the same entity name. Multiple conflicting names dilute brand clarity and confuse entity attribution.
CR4.remedy: Consolidate all Organization schemas into a single, consistent name value that reflects the institution’s true identity.
CR4.risk: Inconsistent schema naming creates identity drift, degrading trust and increasing the likelihood of entity split or misclassification.

CR5.title: Canonical Root Singularity
CR5.impact: AI engines require a single canonical root to assign institutional authority.
CR5.meaning: Only one root canonical domain may be asserted. Multiple variations dilute the ability to infer where the official identity originates.
CR5.remedy: Remove secondary canonicals and enforce a single normalized homepage canonical representing the true root entity.
CR5.risk: Models may treat the entity as two or more separate institutions, weakening visibility, authority weight, and graph cohesion.

CR6.title: Entity Name Presence
CR6.impact: An entity cannot be reconstructed without explicit naming signals.
CR6.meaning: If schema objects indicate the presence of an entity (Organization or Person), an entityName must exist to anchor identity.
CR6.remedy: Declare a clear entity name in either Organization schemas, title prefix, or derived entity metadata.
CR6.risk: Without a stable identifier, AI is forced to infer identity heuristically, which reduces interpretability and reconstructive accuracy.

CR7.title: Schema Vocabulary Standardization
CR7.impact: AI-grade parsing expects structural facts to be encoded in schema.org vocabulary.
CR7.meaning: All JSON-LD blocks must use schema.org namespaces, ensuring consistent machine interpretability and knowledge-graph ingestion.
CR7.remedy: Update structured data to reflect schema.org contexts and remove non-schema.org formats or proprietary variants.
CR7.risk: Structured data that deviates from standard vocabulary is ignored or misinterpreted by crawlers, reducing trust signals.

CR8.title: Person–Organization Identity Non-Conflict
CR8.impact: Conflicting Person and Organization schemas imply unclear ownership or misassigned identity.
CR8.meaning: If Person schema exists, it must not conflict with Organization identity. The page must clearly separate institutional identity from individual attribution.
CR8.remedy: Ensure Person nodes represent authorship or contributors without duplicating or competing with the Organization identity.
CR8.risk: Identity collision may lead to ambiguous graph resolution, causing AI to assign ownership to individuals rather than the institution.

CR9.title: Breadcrumb Structural Validity
CR9.impact: A logical breadcrumb hierarchy is a key trust signal that reveals how the entity organizes itself.
CR9.meaning: Breadcrumb position values must follow a strict sequential order to confirm the hierarchy is intentional and coherent.
CR9.remedy: Correct breadcrumb item positions to begin at 1 and increment consecutively without gaps.
CR9.risk: Invalid breadcrumb structure may be interpreted as auto-generated noise and excluded from entity modeling.

CR10.title: Anti-Generic Canonical Enforcement
CR10.impact: AI models treat query strings, hashes, and tracking parameters as unstable surfaces.
CR10.meaning: A canonical must represent a pure institutional root free from query parameters or tracking fragments.
CR10.remedy: Strip all parameters, ensure canonical represents a clean root, and avoid UTM/marketing variants in canonical declarations.
CR10.risk: Dirty canonicals weaken authority and may cause AI systems to disregard canonical intent completely.

CR11.title: Unified Identity Declaration
CR11.impact: AI expects a single, stable expression of entity identity across all top-level signals.
CR11.meaning: Identity roots (hostname, canonical root, schema identity root) must not contradict each other.
CR11.remedy: Normalize hostname-level identity, canonical declarations, and schema name roots to a single coherent value.
CR11.risk: Conflicting roots reduce graph confidence and weaken authority propagation across surfaces.

CR12.title: Hostname Alignment Check
CR12.impact: The hostname must reinforce entity identity, not contradict it.
CR12.meaning: If hostname normalization disagrees with the inferred entity root, crawlers may doubt the legitimacy of the domain as the authoritative surface.
CR12.remedy: Ensure that the canonical identity root and hostname normalization resolve to the same effective domain value.
CR12.risk: Disparity can signal staging environments, cloned sites, or identity drift scenarios.

CR13.title: Minimum Narrative Depth
CR13.impact: AI models evaluate meaningfulness and institutional intent by narrative completeness.
CR13.meaning: The page must contain at least moderate content volume to allow entity reconstruction and semantic inference.
CR13.remedy: Expand visible content with descriptive, substantive text that communicates identity, purpose, or scope.
CR13.risk: Sparse pages are treated as weak surfaces with insufficient evidence to support entity trust weighting.

CR14.title: Internal Lattice Access
CR14.impact: Internal navigation confirms the entity controls a meaningful information perimeter.
CR14.meaning: At least one internal path must be present to demonstrate the existence of a broader institutional lattice beyond the current page.
CR14.remedy: Link to relevant internal hubs, articles, or structured subpages that form an identifiable graph.
CR14.risk: Without internal links, AI treats the surface as isolated and structurally insignificant.

CR15.title: Schema-Based Entity Declaration
CR15.impact: Schema nodes representing Organization or Person identity are the foundation of machine-readable entity trust.
CR15.meaning: At least one top-level identity schema must appear for the page to be considered a candidate for EEI subject ranking.
CR15.remedy: Add an Organization or Person JSON-LD definition that clearly declares the entity and its attributes.
CR15.risk: Without explicit schema nodes, the entity cannot be formally reconstructed in search layers.

CR16.title: Canonical Convergence
CR16.impact: A stable surface perimeter requires the canonical to converge to a single authoritative root.
CR16.meaning: If multiple canonical roots exist, crawlers cannot determine where institutional authority is anchored.
CR16.remedy: Enforce uniform canonical declarations that resolve back to the same normalized registrable root domain.
CR16.risk: Diffused roots lead to uncertain graph propagation, weakened identity cohesion, and poorer institutional clarity.

CR17.title: Multi-Surface Schema Coherence
CR17.impact: Entities represented across social, outbound, and sameAs surfaces must maintain schema-anchored identity.
CR17.meaning: When multiple surfaces exist, Organization or Person schema must be present to anchor cross-surface identity to a single institution.
CR17.remedy: Add schema that formally declares the entity identity and ensure its attributes match each visible surface.
CR17.risk: Without schema, AI may treat surface diversity as fragmentation rather than institutional reach.

CR18.title: Surface Overlap Trust Signal
CR18.impact: Overlap between outbound social links and declared sameAs URLs confirms cross-surface identity alignment.
CR18.meaning: Identifiable surfaces must substantially intersect to prove continuity between schema, social profiles, and outbound references.
CR18.remedy: Ensure declared sameAs surfaces correspond to the same major social hosts referenced from the page.
CR18.risk: Weak overlap signals fragmented presence and lowers trust in the cohesiveness of the institution's digital perimeter.

CR19.title: Schema Root Token Unification
CR19.impact: Entity roots derived from schema attributes must converge on a single normalized token.
CR19.meaning: LegalName, Organization.name, alternateName, and entityName must not produce multiple normalized identity roots.
CR19.remedy: Standardize naming fields to eliminate divergent identity roots and represent the institution consistently.
CR19.risk: Multiple schema roots weaken machine certainty and increase risk of identity bifurcation.

CR20.title: Digital Perimeter Stability
CR20.impact: Stable institutional identity requires coherent convergence across canonical, schema roots, and multi-surface identity signals.
CR20.meaning: A digitally stable perimeter reflects unified root domains, overlapping external surfaces, and consistent schema identity.
CR20.remedy: Align canonical declarations, schema identity, sameAs, and outbound hosts so that each surface reflects the same entity root.
CR20.risk: Instability signals weak institutional identity, degrading authority, reconstruction confidence, and cross-surface trust alignment.
